title: The False Alibi
description: Two wrong publications and a suspicious R-aspect article. The naive plan wastes a step — but one well-chosen master mix removes two targets at once and reveals why the article is wrong.

hints.text: Three targets: ing1's publication (claims Ppn), ing3's publication (claims Nnp), and Art-1 (R aspect, partially wrong). The minimum plan for the publications is two master mixes. ing1 is definitively known — use it as your witness for both steps.

Mix ing1 + ing5. True result: R−.
ing1's publication claims Ppn. Predicted mix(Ppn + Nnp) = neutral ≠ R−, conflict is unambiguous → ing1's publication removed. As a bonus: the R− result confirms ing5 is R−, directly contradicting Art-1's claim that ing5 is R+ → Art-1 also cleared.

Now mix ing1 + ing3. True result: B−.
ing3's publication claims Nnp. Predicted mix(npN + Nnp) = R− ≠ B−, conflict is unambiguous → ing3's publication removed.

Step 1: Master — mix ing1 + ing5 → true R−. Claimed Ppn for ing1: mix(Ppn, Nnp) = neutral ≠ R− → ing1's publication removed. (Bonus: R− result with known ing5 confirms ing5 is R−, contradicting Art-1's R+ claim → Art-1 cleared.)
Step 2: Master — mix ing1 + ing3 → true B−. Claimed Nnp for ing3: mix(npN, Nnp) = R− ≠ B− → ing3's publication removed.

Q2 (conflict only, fixed ing1): mix ing1 + ing3 → true B−. Both publications are in conflict — neither can be unambiguously blamed → neither removed. Pure conflict.
